title: Allan govt unveils $14m rescue package as it braces for AI-driven job losses
summary: The Allan government has announced a $14 million package to help Victorian workers facing job losses due to AI. The plan includes an $8.2 million career rescue scheme and a new TAFE centre to support those in at-risk industries.
ticker_line: Victoria launches $14m AI job rescue package
author: Alex White
heading: AI

title: NAB fixed interest rate hike signals more pain to come for borrowers
summary: National Australia Bank (NAB) has raised its fixed mortgage rates by 15 basis points, indicating potential further interest rate hikes ahead. The lowest fixed rate is now 6.49%, and forecasts suggest rates could rise again by August as the Reserve Bank of Australia continues to combat inflation.
ticker_line: NAB raises fixed mortgage rates amid inflation fight
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Banking

title: Super not an answer to all your tax issues
summary: Recent budget changes have made superannuation a more tax-friendly investment, but it may not be suitable for everyone due to strict withdrawal rules and new taxes on high balances. Many Australians still face financial pressures that make super less appealing for younger age groups.
ticker_line: Superannuation gains but still not for all
author: ANTHONY KEANE
heading: Superannuation

title: Missing flood data hits 25,000
summary: Over 25,000 property owners in Logan have been informed that council flood maps over-estimated water levels by 1 metre. This finding, from an independent review, has raised concerns about property values and insurance costs.
ticker_line: Logan flood maps over-estimate water levels by 1m
author: Judith Kerr
heading: General Insurance

title: Forget banks, super funds are now too big to fail
summary: Australia's financial system passed a stress test that highlighted risks between banks and super funds, revealing their deep connections. With super funds now holding over $4 trillion, they are becoming too big to fail, raising concerns about potential market stress during economic downturns.
ticker_line: Super funds now seen as too big to fail
author: Eric Johnston
heading: Superannuation

title: Closing Bell: Near-zero rates are closer than you think
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia is preparing for the possibility of near-zero interest rates as economic growth slows. New guidelines will help the RBA respond more effectively to future economic crises by outlining various monetary policy tools beyond just adjusting the cash rate.
ticker_line: RBA prepares for potential near-zero interest rates
author: Anthony Macdonald
heading: Economy

title: Economists douse inflation hopes
summary: Economists warn that inflation in Australia remains high despite Treasurer Jim Chalmers' optimism, with underlying inflation rising and concerns about fuel prices and economic growth persisting. The Reserve Bank may consider another interest rate hike as the government’s fiscal policies clash with efforts to control inflation.
ticker_line: Economists caution on rising inflation risks
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Economy

title: Mandatory registration begins for NDIS providers
summary: Today marks the start of mandatory registration for NDIS providers, focusing on digital platforms and supported independent living homes. Concerns are rising among small providers about the costs and future changes expected next year.
ticker_line: NDIS providers face new registration rules
heading: Regulation

title: Bill shock has CEOs eyeing cheaper AI models
summary: Westpac's CEO Anthony Miller is cautious about rising costs from using AI, prompting interest in cheaper Chinese AI models. This shift could spark a price war among major US AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic.
ticker_line: Westpac CEOs cautious of rising AI costs
author: Paul Smith | Technology
heading: AI

title: Corporates revolt over AI bill shock
summary: Companies are facing rising costs from using AI tools, leading to a demand for cheaper Chinese models. This price competition is challenging US firms like OpenAI and Anthropic as they prepare for public listings.
ticker_line: Corporates struggle with rising AI costs
author: Paul Smith
heading: AI

title: June sees biggest monthly fall in national housing values since 2022, Cotality finds
summary: In June 2026, national housing values fell by 0.4%, marking the largest monthly drop since 2022, with Sydney and Melbourne experiencing the biggest declines. Factors such as rising interest rates, tax changes, and low affordability are contributing to this downturn in the housing market.
ticker_line: National housing values see largest drop since 2022
author: business Lin Lin
heading: Economy

title: Rise of industry funds: shake-up ahead for Aussie super
summary: Deloitte predicts that industry super funds will grow from 46% to 55% of Australia's superannuation market by 2045, as platform investments and self-managed funds decline. This shift comes after a report highlighted issues in the platform market, including rising advice fees and the collapse of certain funds, affecting many Australians' savings.
ticker_line: Industry super funds set for major growth
author: Kaaren Morrissey
heading: Superannuation

title: RBA readies for economy 'fire drills'
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia plans to cut interest rates more aggressively in future economic downturns instead of relying on unconventional methods like printing money. Assistant Governor Christopher Kent highlighted the need for better preparedness for economic shocks, following lessons learned from the pandemic.
ticker_line: RBA prepares for future economic challenges
author: Shane Wright | Senior economics cor
heading: Economy

title: It’s not our fault, says Labor
summary: Labor is facing criticism for claiming that high interest rates are mainly to blame for low auction clearance rates, while downplaying the impact of its tax reforms. Recent data shows clearance rates have dropped to a six-year low of 49.2%, raising concerns about the housing market and future rent pressures.
ticker_line: Labor blames interest rates for low auction clearance
author: Jessica Wang
heading: Economy

title: NAB calls for national strategy to tackle increase in mortgage fraud
summary: NAB is urging for a national strategy to combat rising mortgage fraud in Australia, estimating $4 billion in fraudulent loans. The bank believes a coordinated approach among banks, regulators, and law enforcement is needed to tackle this widespread issue effectively.
ticker_line: NAB calls for national strategy against mortgage fraud
author: Blair Jackson
heading: Banking

title: ASIC ‘asleep at the wheel’ over junkets
summary: Former senator John Williams criticises ASIC for not addressing the return of lavish perks in financial advice, linked to The Inside Network, which connects fund managers with advisers. He urges ASIC to investigate potential wrongdoing after reforms aimed at preventing conflicts of interest in the industry.
ticker_line: ASIC faces criticism over financial perks issue
author: ELI GREENBLAT
heading: Regulation

title: NDIS fraudsters heading to prison in taskforce blitz
summary: Seventeen people involved in NDIS fraud have been sentenced to over 60 years in prison. The Fraud Fusion Taskforce is actively investigating more cases to recover billions lost to fraud.
ticker_line: NDIS fraudsters sentenced to over 60 years in jail
author: Jessica Wang
heading: Regulation

title: BIS: Investment boom in AI could leave economy in peril
summary: The Bank for International Settlements warns that excessive investment in AI by major companies could lead to economic downturns if profits disappoint, similar to past technology booms. They highlight risks of overinvestment and potential financial instability due to reliance on borrowing for AI projects.
ticker_line: BIS warns of AI investment risks to economy
author: PAUL HANNON
heading: Economy

title: AI Data Centers Now Account for 17% of Australia’s Private Investment
summary: AI data centers now make up 17% of all private investment in Australia, highlighting the growing importance of artificial intelligence in the country's economy. This shift reflects a significant increase in funding and interest in AI technologies.
ticker_line: AI data centers surge to 17% of investment
heading: AI

title: The data centre mega-hub being quietly mapped out in Melbourne’s west
summary: A large data centre hub, called the Victorian AI Hub, is planned for Plumpton, Melbourne, covering 350 hectares and aiming for 2.4 gigawatts of power capacity. The project, developed by Syncline Energy, is expected to create thousands of jobs and support Victoria's tech industry, but it faces planning challenges due to its size and location.
ticker_line: Massive data centre planned for Melbourne's west
author: Daniella White
heading: AI

title: Macquarie could disrupt big four's 'lazy' $370b edge
summary: Macquarie is challenging Australia's big four banks by offering higher interest rates on savings, which could significantly impact the banks' profits. Analysts warn that if the major banks respond by raising rates, their earnings could drop by up to 40%.
ticker_line: Macquarie disrupts big banks with higher savings rates
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: Labor's new homes numbers $14b off RBA's
summary: The federal budget predicts $13.7 billion more investment in new housing than the Reserve Bank, suggesting the government may have overestimated housing supply by over 10,000 homes. This discrepancy raises concerns about meeting Labor's goal of 1.2 million new homes.
ticker_line: Budget housing investment forecasts raise concerns
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Economy

title: HESTA backs $70m VC fund for med tech stars
summary: HESTA is investing $70 million in Synthesis Capital, a new venture capital firm focused on early-stage Australian health and medical technology start-ups. The fund aims to connect local scientists with global investors and address funding challenges in the sector.
ticker_line: HESTA invests $70m in med tech venture fund
author: Tess Bennett
heading: Investments

title: Social impact investors 'can fix NDIS'
summary: Michael Traill proposes a major change to the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) by consolidating its 270,000 providers into a few large operators backed by social impact investors to improve efficiency and save costs. This plan aims to address the scheme's financial issues and achieve $185 billion in budget savings over the next decade.
ticker_line: Traill's bold plan to reform NDIS funding
author: Michael Smith | Health
heading: General Insurance

title: Economic gap between older and younger generations on track to reach record levels
summary: A report shows the economic gap between younger and older Australians is growing, with younger people struggling more with home ownership and stagnant wages. This inequality could reach record levels in the coming years.
ticker_line: Economic gap between generations set to widen
author: business Adelaide Miller
heading: Economy

title: Bendigo deal on union’s hit list
summary: Bendigo Bank plans to transfer hundreds of tech workers to Infosys, but unions warn the new contracts could strip away basic rights and worsen pay conditions. The Finance Sector Union is advising workers not to sign these agreements.
ticker_line: Bendigo Bank's tech workers face contract concerns
author: Rishab Jain
heading: Banking

title: Cancer serious but falls short of policy definition
summary: A man has lost his claim for trauma benefits from Zurich after the Australian Financial Complaints Authority ruled his cancer did not meet the policy's strict definition, despite being a serious condition. The policy specifically excluded his type of cancer, which was classified as non-invasive.
ticker_line: Man's cancer claim denied by Zurich insurer
heading: Life Insurance

title: Queensland ‘loses out’ under disaster funding shake-up
summary: Queensland may lose $500 million a year due to proposed federal changes to disaster funding, which will shift from a 75% federal contribution to a 50:50 split. This could lead to delays in repairs for local councils, especially in regional areas, as they struggle with high disaster costs.
ticker_line: Queensland faces $500 million loss in disaster funding
heading: General Insurance

title: Bigger buffer on household line shows ‘underlying uncertainty’
summary: Insurers are increasing their financial buffers for household insurance due to rising uncertainties like natural disasters and inflation. The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority's latest data shows risk margins for household insurance have grown since 2013, reflecting a more cautious approach to unexpected claims.
ticker_line: Insurers raise buffers amid rising uncertainties
heading: General Insurance

title: ICA levy call as NSW annual insurance haul tops $3b
summary: New South Wales will collect over $3 billion annually from insurance levies and duties, with emergency services levy expected to rise significantly. The Insurance Council of Australia urges for reform to reduce the financial burden on homeowners and businesses.
ticker_line: NSW insurance levies exceed $3 billion annually
heading: General Insurance

title: High-stakes code review still has some way to run
summary: The Insurance Council of Australia is updating its code of practice, which will now be part of customer contracts and needs approval from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission. Key changes include automatic acceptance of home and motor claims after 12 months and new definitions for vulnerability and urgent financial needs, with submissions on the draft due by July 21.
ticker_line: Insurance Council updates code for better consumer protection
heading: Regulation

title: ASIC slams super safety failures
summary: ASIC has found that superannuation trustees are failing to protect member savings, highlighting serious issues with monitoring fees and risky strategies. The regulator warns that immediate improvements are needed to avoid enforcement action.
ticker_line: ASIC warns super trustees on safety failures
author: JOSEPH CARBONE
heading: Superannuation

title: Private health group Ramsay given special access to public hospital emergency patients
summary: Ramsay Health Care has been allowed to transfer privately insured patients from a Queensland public hospital's emergency department to its private facilities, raising concerns about patient pressure and fairness in healthcare access. The trial, which has already moved over 2,000 patients, is set to expand nationally.
ticker_line: Ramsay gains access to public hospital emergency patients
author: business Alison Branley
heading: Health Insurance

title: Post-COVID debt growth challenges all but one state
summary: Most Australian states and territories face rising debt and interest costs, forcing them to consider cuts to services. Only Western Australia is not under similar pressure, while overall state debt is expected to reach nearly $1 trillion by 2030.
ticker_line: States struggle with soaring debt and interest costs
author: Shane Wright | Senior economics cor
heading: Economy

title: AI bonanza doesn’t add up for Qld
summary: Queensland is opposing the Albanese government's $150 billion data centre plan, raising concerns about costs and energy reliability. The state wants more details before agreeing to national energy proposals that could affect electricity prices for residents.
ticker_line: Queensland challenges federal data centre energy plan
author: PERRY WILLIAMS, CHIEF BU
heading: Energy

title: Inside the data governance crisis in the public service
summary: A recent study found that 67% of public service employees struggle to understand their organisations' data, highlighting serious gaps in data management and governance. Many agencies are failing to complete important metadata fields, risking the security and proper use of sensitive information.
ticker_line: Public service data governance crisis revealed
author: Ray Athwal
heading: Regulation

title: DATA CRISIS EXPOSED
summary: A survey reveals that one in three public-sector data professionals distrust their own data, with many lacking tools to manage it properly. This comes amid serious data management issues, highlighting gaps in data governance and the need for better documentation and oversight.
ticker_line: Public sector data trust issues revealed in survey
author: Ray Athwal
heading: Regulation

title: Rate hold expectations on slowdown
summary: Inflation in Australia slowed to 4.2% in April, partly due to the government's fuel excise cut, leading economists to expect the Reserve Bank to keep interest rates steady in June. However, rising costs in housing and transport, along with ongoing global issues, may continue to challenge inflation.
ticker_line: Inflation slows, interest rates likely to hold
author: Lea Jurkovic | Economics correspond
heading: Economy

title: Big fine for big four bank
summary: Westpac has been fined $26 million by the Federal Court for not properly handling over 200 customer requests for financial help between 2017 and 2023. The court found the bank was grossly negligent in its response to customers facing hardships like job loss and illness.
ticker_line: Westpac fined $26 million for customer neglect
author: David Hannant
heading: Banking

title: RBA’s dilemma: Fight inflation or save growth?
summary: Australia's economy is slowing down while inflation stays high, creating a tough situation for the Reserve Bank. With rising unemployment and concerns over new tax changes, the bank may need to decide soon whether to raise interest rates or keep them steady.
ticker_line: RBA faces tough choices amid slowing economy
author: David Rogers, markets
heading: Economy

title: Inflation dip may hold off rate rise
summary: Inflation in Australia has dropped to 4.2% in April, down from 4.6% in March, which may lead the Reserve Bank to pause interest rate hikes next month. However, ongoing global conflicts could still impact future inflation and rate decisions.
ticker_line: Inflation drop may pause interest rate hikes
author: Duncan Evans
heading: Economy

title: Audit reveals cyberattack fears over this year's census
summary: An audit has revealed that the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) needs to fix important cybersecurity issues before the upcoming census on August 11, 2026. The report highlights that while some improvements have been made, there are still critical gaps that could leave the system vulnerable to cyberattacks.
ticker_line: Census cybersecurity concerns raised ahead of August survey
author: Rob Harris | National correspondent
heading: AI

title: NSW teens’ tech literacy falling sharply
summary: IT literacy among 15 to 16-year-olds in NSW has dropped to record lows, with only 37% achieving a proficient standard in 2025, down from 44% in 2022. This decline reflects a national trend, despite many students reporting regular use of technology and AI.
ticker_line: NSW teens' IT skills hit record low
author: Will Paige and Mary Papadakis
heading: AI

title: 36yo left shocked at peak tax age realisation
summary: A new report shows that Australians aged 30 pay significantly more tax than those aged 70, despite earning the same income, highlighting a major wealth gap in the tax system. The findings reveal that middle-aged workers are the biggest taxpayers, while retirees benefit from government support.
ticker_line: Tax burden shifts from young to old Australians
author: Ben Graham
heading: Economy

title: ‘Deeply concerning’: ASIC warns on gaps in oversight of $300b in super
summary: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has raised concerns about the lack of oversight in the superannuation sector, specifically regarding excessive fees charged by financial advisers on platforms managing $300 billion in member funds. ASIC's review found that trustees are not adequately protecting members' retirement savings, especially those with low balances, and highlighted the need for better monitoring of fees and adviser practices.
ticker_line: ASIC warns on superannuation fee oversight gaps
author: Clancy Yeates
heading: Superannuation

title: ASIC says too many Australians' retirement savings being wiped out
summary: ASIC warns that many Australians' retirement savings are at risk due to poor oversight by superannuation trustees, following the loss of $1 billion from the collapse of Shield and First Guardian. The watchdog urges trustees to improve safeguards and for Australians to engage more with their superannuation.
ticker_line: ASIC raises alarm over retirement savings safety
author: business correspondent David Taylor
heading: Superannuation

title: Insurance gap leaves home builders ruined
summary: Michael Nelson faces a $300,000 loss after his builder went bankrupt, leaving his home renovation unfinished. He is unable to access the increased insurance coverage due to starting his project before the new rules took effect.
ticker_line: Builder insolvency leaves homeowners in crisis
author: Georgina Sebar
heading: General Insurance

title: Bosses failing to offer AI training for workers
summary: A survey shows that nearly 80% of workers using AI have not received any formal training from their employers, raising concerns about companies' readiness to adapt to new technologies. While many employees recognise the need for AI skills, employers are struggling to provide the necessary training and support.
ticker_line: Workers lack AI training as companies lag behind
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: AI

title: House prices on road to nowhere, or ‘modest’ squeeze
summary: Macquarie analysts predict a possible decline in Australian house prices by 5% due to recent tax changes by the Albanese government, which may lead to a long period of stagnant prices. They warn that investor activity in the housing market could drop significantly, affecting new home lending and overall market turnover.
ticker_line: House prices face potential 5% decline
author: David Ross
heading: Economy

title: Australia:A sustainable CSLR needs structural reform, says Insurance Council
summary: The Insurance Council of Australia is urging for major changes to the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR) to ensure its long-term sustainability, rather than just finding new funding methods. They believe reforms should focus on the root causes of costs and ensure that only those responsible for misconduct fund the compensation.
ticker_line: Insurance Council pushes for CSLR reform
author: Jimmy John
heading: General Insurance

title: Return to rural banking is a growth story for Westpac
summary: Westpac is reopening branches in rural Australia, including Moree, to better serve local communities and farmers. This move comes after years of branch closures, highlighting the importance of personal banking relationships in agriculture.
ticker_line: Westpac expands rural banking network for growth
author: Tim Johnston
heading: Banking

title: ASX hits the skids as costs up 21pc
summary: ASX shares fell 13% after the company announced a cost increase of up to 21% next year due to higher technology expenses and regulatory issues. This follows criticism from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission for prioritising profits over market stability.
ticker_line: ASX shares plunge as costs soar 21%
author: Cliona O’Dowd
heading: Banking

title: ‘No guarantees’ on Labor’s NDIS bill
summary: Opposition NDIS spokeswoman Melissa McIntosh warns there are 'no guarantees' the Coalition will support Labor's plan to cut $185 billion from the NDIS over the next decade, raising concerns about potential unfair cuts to participants. Any delays in passing the legislation could jeopardise the government's budget goals.
ticker_line: Coalition's support for NDIS cuts uncertain
author: Sarah Ison, Senior political report
heading: General Insurance

title: Data centres can wind up energy
summary: Energy Minister Chris Bowen believes Australia's data centre industry can help boost renewable energy projects, especially wind farms, as the country struggles to meet its 2030 renewable energy targets. With data centres expected to double their energy needs by 2030, new rules may be needed to ensure they contribute positively to the energy grid.
ticker_line: Data centres to aid Australia's renewable energy goals
author: Perry Williams, Elizabeth Pike
heading: AI

title: Tailored training and a trade focus means no one need be left behind
summary: AI has the potential to improve productivity for Australia's skilled tradespeople by reducing administrative tasks and enhancing safety, but it requires proper training and worker involvement for successful adoption.
ticker_line: AI could boost productivity for tradespeople
author: Steven Miller
heading: AI

title: AI’s economic gains proving elusive: Altman
summary: OpenAI's CEO Sam Altman says the expected economic benefits of artificial intelligence are harder to achieve than anticipated, impacting jobs in unexpected ways. He highlighted recent job cuts at companies like CBA and Block due to AI, while also noting Australia's potential as a global data centre hub.
ticker_line: AI's economic impact harder to achieve, says Altman
author: David Ross
heading: AI

title: Goodman upbeat on race for AI riches
summary: Billionaire Greg Goodman believes Australia can benefit from the global AI investment boom, which could be worth up to $1.4 trillion. He highlights the need for better infrastructure and affordable power to attract AI companies and meet growing demand for data centres.
ticker_line: Goodman optimistic about Australia's AI future
author: Ben Wilmot
heading: AI

title: Business council out of step with youth on AI
summary: Australia's business leaders warn that failing to prioritise AI and data centres could cost the economy $1.4 trillion, but many young people want more regulation due to job fears. The debate intensifies as major tech companies announce job cuts while investing heavily in AI infrastructure.
ticker_line: AI investment debate divides business and youth
author: Tansy Harcourt
heading: AI

title: Why big super is investing your money in AI despite ‘biggest risk’
summary: Aware Super, a large Australian superannuation fund, is investing in AI despite concerns about high valuations and market risks. The fund recently participated in SpaceX's IPO but is cautious about future investments in other AI companies like Anthropic and OpenAI due to potential market volatility.
ticker_line: Aware Super invests in AI amid market risks
author: Cliona O’Dowd
heading: Superannuation

title: Super funds freeze dead partners’ cash
summary: Grieving families face long delays of over six months to access death benefit payouts from superannuation funds, causing financial stress. A review by ASIC reveals slow progress in improving these processes, with calls for better communication and mandatory service standards.
ticker_line: Super funds delay death benefit payouts for families
author: David Bonaddio
heading: Superannuation

title: Home truths for SMSF property investors in race to beat the ban
summary: New laws will ban self-managed super funds from borrowing to buy residential properties, effective mid-August 2026. Investors must act quickly to secure properties before the deadline, while commercial property purchases remain unaffected.
ticker_line: SMSF property investment ban set for August
author: JAMES GERRARD, ANALYSIS
heading: Superannuation

title: Commonwealth Bank boss Matt Comyn says government should reconsider CGT on non-passive assets
summary: Commonwealth Bank CEO Matt Comyn has urged the government to limit capital gains tax changes to passive assets like housing, arguing that broader applications could discourage investment and innovation. He plans to discuss these concerns with Treasurer Jim Chalmers soon.
ticker_line: CBA CEO calls for targeted capital gains tax reform
heading: Banking

title: Investors on shaky ground as data points to a market slowdown
summary: Investors are facing challenges as new federal budget changes, including the removal of negative gearing for existing properties, are expected to reduce demand and lending in the housing market. Data shows a decline in housing loan commitments and a drop in consumer confidence, particularly among owner-occupiers.
ticker_line: Market slowdown as investor demand weakens
author: Nina Hendy
heading: Economy

title: Call to ban lead generators for super
summary: The Super Members Council is calling for a ban on lead generators in superannuation to protect Australians from fraud and unsafe financial practices. They urge the government to strengthen regulations and complete reforms to financial advice to ensure better safety for retirement savings.
ticker_line: Call to ban lead generators in superannuation
author: David Ross
heading: Superannuation

title: The AI experiment is over: Australian leaders now have to move
summary: Australian leaders must quickly decide which AI programs to scale up, as the trial phase is over and the country is lagging behind global trends in AI investment and adoption. Effective organisational change and clear standards are essential for successful AI integration.
ticker_line: Australian leaders urged to scale AI quickly
author: Bec McConnochie
heading: AI

title: Australia, UK tackle AI safety
summary: Australia and the UK have signed an agreement to share information on artificial intelligence risks, even before Australia establishes its own AI safety watchdog. This partnership aims to enhance research and responses to AI threats.
ticker_line: Australia and UK unite on AI safety measures
author: Jared Lynch
heading: AI

title: Insurance Council of Australia launches consultation on revised General Insurance Code
summary: The Insurance Council of Australia is seeking public feedback on a revised General Insurance Code aimed at improving consumer protections and customer service. Key changes include making the Code legally enforceable, automatic acceptance of certain claims after 12 months, and better support for vulnerable customers.
ticker_line: ICA seeks feedback on new insurance code
author: Jake Nathanoel Dellosa
heading: General Insurance

title: Jobs and spending data put rate rise back in play
summary: Australia's jobless rate fell to 4.4% and household spending rose 1.3% in May, suggesting the economy is still strong and may lead to more interest rate hikes by the Reserve Bank. The data shows resilience in spending despite concerns about inflation and cost-of-living challenges.
ticker_line: Strong jobs and spending data may prompt rate hikes
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Economy

title: UniSuper, Hostplus lead super thrivers
summary: UniSuper and Hostplus are leading Australia's super funds with high-growth returns of 12.8% and 12.3% respectively, as the financial year ends. Other funds like AustralianSuper and HESTA also performed well, but some lagged behind with lower returns amid global market volatility.
ticker_line: UniSuper and Hostplus top super fund returns
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Unemployment drops but labour market cracks emerge
summary: Australia's unemployment rate fell to 4.4% with over 40,000 jobs added in May, but revisions show previous job losses and a drop in job vacancies indicate a weakening labour market. Economists expect the unemployment rate to rise to 5% by early 2027 due to slower hiring.
ticker_line: Unemployment drops but labour market shows cracks
author: Thomas Henry, Matthew Cranston
heading: Economy

title: Risky business: Life insurers aren’t just investors in private credit. They’re lenders, too
summary: A report shows that many life insurers are not only investing in private-credit funds but also lending money to them, increasing both potential returns and risks. This double exposure could lead to significant losses if loans default, raising concerns about the stability of the insurance sector.
ticker_line: Life insurers double down on risky private credit
author: MATT WIRZ, SHANE SHIFFLETT
heading: Life Insurance

title: ASIC hails progress on red tape reduction
summary: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has made significant progress in reducing red tape in financial services and is moving to the next phase of its simplification program. Recent changes include e-lodgement for forms and improved consultation with the industry to ease reporting burdens.
ticker_line: ASIC advances red tape reduction in finance
heading: Regulation

title: Global turmoil prompts APRA to step up supervision
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is increasing its supervision of banks, insurers, and superannuation funds due to global disruptions affecting supply chains and investment risks. While the financial system is stable, APRA is concerned about the growing links between private markets and regulated sectors, especially with life insurers investing in riskier assets.
ticker_line: APRA boosts oversight amid global financial risks
heading: Regulation

title: State maintains freeze on workers’ comp rates
summary: Victoria will keep its average WorkCover premium at 1.8% of wages for the fourth consecutive year in 2026-27. The government is focusing on improving support for injured workers, especially in high-risk jobs, without raising costs for businesses.
ticker_line: Victoria freezes workers' comp rates for 2026-27
heading: General Insurance

title: Home coverage warning as new smoke alarm rules loom
summary: Queensland homeowners must install new interconnected photoelectric smoke alarms by 2027 to keep their insurance valid. Failure to comply could lead to denied fire damage claims.
ticker_line: Queensland smoke alarm rules could impact insurance
heading: General Insurance

title: Three key innovation trends, according to ASIC
summary: ASIC's new report highlights three major trends in insurance innovation: the use of AI in claims and underwriting, the rise of embedded insurance products, and the growth of parametric and usage-based insurance. These trends are reshaping how insurance is distributed and managed in Australia.
ticker_line: ASIC outlines top trends in insurance innovation
heading: AI

title: Mortgage pain spreads to affluent suburbs
summary: Mortgage stress is now affecting wealthy suburbs as changes to property tax rules put pressure on homeowners and investors. This could lead to falling house prices and rising rents, especially in high-demand areas.
ticker_line: Mortgage stress hits wealthy suburbs hard
author: Ben Wilmot
heading: Banking

title: ‘We are risking a $1.4 trillion boom going bust’
summary: Australia risks missing out on a $1.4 trillion AI investment boom unless the government acts quickly to attract capital and streamline regulations, according to Business Council of Australia chief executive Bran Black. He urges a national commitment to make AI and data centre investments a priority to boost productivity and living standards.
ticker_line: Australia urged to seize $1.4 trillion AI opportunity
author: JOE KELLY, WASHINGTON CORRESPONDENT
heading: AI

title: SMSF lending ban contradicts the budget it was bolted onto
summary: The Australian government plans to ban new lending for residential property within self-managed super funds (SMSFs) to improve housing affordability, but this move may actually reduce housing supply and limit retirement options for many Australians. The ban will take effect 45 days after legislation is passed, despite not being included in the budget or consulted on beforehand.
ticker_line: Government bans SMSF property lending to boost affordability
author: Diane Tate
heading: Superannuation

title: CPI figures keep rate rises on table
summary: Australia's inflation rate fell to 4% in May, helped by a temporary fuel tax cut, but a rise in underlying inflation to 3.6% suggests the Reserve Bank may raise interest rates again this year. The RBA held the cash rate at 4.35% this month, but experts predict at least one more increase is likely in August.
ticker_line: Inflation drop raises interest rate concerns
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Economy

title: Banks face big AI-led risks: APRA
summary: Australia's banking regulator, APRA, warns that risks from advanced AI models, especially after the Trump administration restricted access to Anthropic's technology, could threaten cybersecurity for major banks. APRA urges banks to share cybersecurity tools and knowledge to strengthen defenses across the financial system.
ticker_line: APRA warns banks of AI cybersecurity risks
author: James Eyers
heading: Regulation

title: Coalition plans to cut taxes for NSW businesses
summary: The Coalition, led by Kellie Sloane, plans to cut payroll taxes for small and medium businesses in NSW to attract more investment and jobs. The proposal raises the tax threshold from $1.2 million to $1.5 million and lowers the tax rate, but could impact state revenue as the government faces declining income from property taxes.
ticker_line: Coalition proposes payroll tax cuts for NSW businesses
author: Jessica McSweeney
heading: Economy

title: Core inflation causes headaches for RBA
summary: Core inflation in Australia rose to 3.6% in May, prompting expectations of another interest rate hike from the Reserve Bank. Despite a drop in headline inflation to 4%, rising costs in housing and electricity remain significant concerns.
ticker_line: Core inflation rise pressures RBA for rate hike
author: THOMAS HENRY MATTHEW CRANSTON A
heading: Economy

title: Hesta loads up amid turmoil
summary: Hesta, a major superannuation fund, has been buying shares in WiseTech Global despite its concerns over the company's leadership and ongoing scandals involving founder Richard White. Hesta has demanded WiseTech address its governance issues after White faced criminal investigations, but still increased its stake in the company.
ticker_line: Hesta increases stake in troubled WiseTech Global
author: Jared Lynch
heading: Superannuation

title: How to use AI – and keep staff levels up
summary: Zeller, a payments platform valued over $1 billion, has used AI to speed up its product development from nine months to under four weeks, while maintaining staff levels. CEO Ben Pfisterer believes AI enhances the roles of software engineers and product developers rather than replacing them, allowing for quicker responses to customer needs.
ticker_line: Zeller speeds up product development with AI
author: JARED LYNCH
heading: AI

title: Data centres not tied to copyright overhaul
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has rejected calls from AI developers for changes to Australia's copyright laws, stating that current regulations are not hindering investment in data centres. Despite concerns from the tech sector, Australia's data centre investment pipeline is strong, exceeding $155 billion.
ticker_line: Chalmers stands firm on copyright laws amid AI push
author: JACK QUAIL
heading: Regulation

title: Aussie bank helping secure ‘lifetime income’
summary: AMP is launching a new program called Lifetime Super Boost, allowing retirees to convert their superannuation into a steady income for life. This initiative aims to help Australians feel more secure about their retirement finances and potentially qualify for more aged pension support.
ticker_line: AMP launches program for secure retirement income
author: NewsWire
heading: Superannuation

title: Sydney, Melbourne tipped to lead housing price drops as market cools
summary: Sydney and Melbourne are expected to see significant drops in housing prices, with Sydney homes potentially losing up to $122,000 and Melbourne's median price falling below $1 million. This decline is driven by higher interest rates and changes in tax policy affecting buyer demand.
ticker_line: Sydney, Melbourne housing prices set to fall
author: business Stephanie Chalmer
heading: Economy

title: RBA looks to get ahead of the curve on jobs
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia is rethinking how unemployment affects inflation, highlighting that low unemployment can lead to sharp wage increases and inflation. Deputy governor Andrew Hauser explains that their new research shows a nonlinear relationship, which influenced recent interest rate hikes to manage inflation pressures.
ticker_line: RBA adjusts strategies to tackle inflation pressures
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: Tweak on super has ‘no defence’
summary: The SMSF Association criticises Labor's decision to ban self-managed funds from borrowing for property investments, saying it reduces investment options and does not solve the real issues. The change was made without consultation and could harm confidence in the superannuation system.
ticker_line: Labor's super change sparks SMSF backlash
heading: Superannuation

title: Victoria’s data centre push could force state back onto gas-fired power
summary: Victoria's growing demand for data centres may force the state to rely more on gas-fired power, as local gas production declines and renewable projects face delays. This situation raises concerns about the state's energy transition plans and potential increases in electricity prices.
ticker_line: Victoria's data centres may increase gas reliance
author: Daniella White
heading: AI

title: ASIC warns of high-interest car loan traps and risky lending practices in new report
summary: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) warns about high-interest car loans and risky lending practices, highlighting cases like Holly Reid's, where borrowers face excessive fees and financial stress. The report reveals that some lenders, especially Rapid Loans, charge extremely high rates and fees, leading to severe consequences for borrowers who struggle to repay their loans.
ticker_line: ASIC warns of risky car loan practices
author: by national consumer affa
heading: Regulation

title: Senior Albanese minister clashes with Pocock over top-secret AI copyright claim
summary: David Pocock and Minister Tim Ayres clashed in Parliament over claims that the government is considering allowing AI companies to use Australian copyright material without proper licensing. Pocock alleges there are two proposals being discussed, while Ayres insists the government will not weaken copyright protections.
ticker_line: Minister clashes with Pocock over AI copyright claims
author: national AI and technology
heading: Regulation

title: RBA in a bind: Fight inflation or risk recession
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia faces a tough choice between raising interest rates to control high inflation or risking a recession as the economy slows. Recent data shows inflation remains high, and while the cash rate is currently at 4.35%, further hikes may be needed if inflation does not decrease significantly.
ticker_line: RBA struggles with inflation and recession risks
author: DAVID ROGERS MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: Payday super poses cash flow challenge
summary: From July 1, employers in Australia must pay superannuation at the same time as salaries, which could create cash flow problems for businesses. Experts warn that this change, aimed at recovering $5.7 billion in unpaid super, will particularly impact small and medium-sized enterprises.
ticker_line: Payday super reform to challenge business cash flow
author: CAMERON MICALLEF
heading: Superannuation

title: Bosses warned of AI cyber onslaught
summary: The Australian Signals Directorate and its Five Eyes partners warn businesses to prepare for a surge in cyber attacks using advanced AI technology, which poses a significant risk to IT networks. Companies are urged to improve their cybersecurity measures and integrate AI into their strategies to combat these evolving threats.
ticker_line: Businesses warned of AI-driven cyber attack risks
author: BEN PACKHAM
heading: AI

title: TPD Sustainability Pressures Topic of Industry Debate
summary: Australia's financial regulators are urging life insurers to take stronger action on sustainability issues in Total Permanent Disability (TPD) insurance, following a recent industry debate. The discussion highlighted increasing mental health claims and the need for product redesign to improve affordability and manage risks.
ticker_line: Regulators push insurers on TPD sustainability challenges
heading: Life Insurance

title: Economy: Traders cut rate rise bets after jobs shock
summary: Traders are less sure the Reserve Bank of Australia will raise interest rates after a surprising rise in unemployment, which suggests the economy may be slowing. While some analysts predict more rate hikes due to ongoing inflation, others believe recent budget changes could reduce the need for further increases.
ticker_line: Traders doubt RBA will raise rates after jobs data
author: Cecile Lefort
heading: Economy

title: Bourse has best day in six weeks
summary: The Australian sharemarket had its best day in six weeks, with the S&P/ASX 200 rising 1.47% to 8621.7, driven by higher unemployment data and a positive lead from Wall Street. Mining stocks led the gains, while the unemployment rate increased to 4.5% in April, suggesting interest rates may stay steady.
ticker_line: Australian sharemarket sees biggest gain in weeks
author: BLAIR JACKSON, EQUITIES
heading: Economy

title: Jobless spike stalls RBA’s next rate hike, for now
summary: Australia's unemployment rate rose to 4.5% in April, leading the Reserve Bank to likely hold interest rates steady at its June meeting. Economists expect a rate hike may still happen by August, depending on future economic data.
ticker_line: Jobless rate rise delays RBA's next rate hike
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: Greens demand Labor tax changes extend to SMSFs
summary: The Greens are pushing Labor to change tax rules for self-managed super funds (SMSFs) and limit borrowing arrangements as part of negotiations over a budget tax bill. They are also concerned that big businesses will benefit from new capital gains tax changes.
ticker_line: Greens demand tax changes for SMSFs in negotiations
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON
heading: Superannuation

title: National Australia Bank deploys AI analytics tools
summary: National Australia Bank (NAB) has launched new AI tools called Genie and Genie code to help users easily query complex data using plain English. These tools aim to simplify data analysis for businesses.
ticker_line: NAB launches AI tools to simplify data queries
heading: AI

title: Australia: Free mental health support used nearly 50,000 in Western Australia
summary: Nearly 50,000 free mental health sessions have been provided in Western Australia through Medicare Mental Health Centres. The Albanese Government plans to expand these centres, investing an additional $29.9 million to improve access to on-call mental health professionals.
ticker_line: Free mental health support reaches 50,000 in WA
heading: Health Insurance

title: Minefield of AI tax returns
summary: Many Australians are using AI tools like ChatGPT to prepare their tax returns, hoping to find more deductions. However, experts warn this could lead to mistakes and penalties from the ATO, as many people are already relying on expected refunds to cover bills and debts.
ticker_line: Australians risk ATO penalties using AI for tax
author: Peter Jenkins
heading: Regulation

title: ASIC Warns of "Lost Generation" Risk if Australia Falls Behind on Fintech and AI
summary: ASIC warns that Australia could face a 'lost generation' if it doesn't keep up with global fintech and AI advancements. The regulator's report highlights the need for better industry engagement and innovation support to avoid a decline in living standards.
ticker_line: ASIC warns of risk to Australia's financial future
publication: Financial and Business News | Finance Magnates
author: Damian Chmiel
heading: Regulation

title: From SpaceX to OpenAI, Australians are piling into Wall St’s mega-deals
summary: Australians are increasingly investing in major US tech companies like SpaceX and OpenAI as local market options dwindle. SpaceX plans to go public in mid-June, potentially raising $80 billion, while interest in related investment funds is growing.
ticker_line: Australians flock to US tech investments
author: Alex Gluyas
heading: AI

title: RBA still stuck between a rock and a hike place
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia may hold interest rates steady next month after recent job losses suggest the economy is slowing, but inflation risks remain high due to ongoing global tensions and rising oil prices.
ticker_line: RBA may pause rate hikes amid job losses
author: James Thomson
heading: Economy

title: CSIRO cuts climate research jobs despite budget funding boost
summary: CSIRO will cut 92 jobs from its climate research teams despite receiving an extra $387.4 million in funding. Critics warn this could weaken Australia's ability to model and predict climate change impacts.
ticker_line: CSIRO cuts climate jobs despite funding boost
author: Bianca Hall and Mike Foley
heading: General Insurance

title: Negative gearing ban forces loan review
summary: Westpac has informed mortgage brokers that it will not honour pre-approved investor loans due to the federal government's ban on negative gearing for existing properties. This change is expected to reduce borrowing capacity for many investors by up to 20%.
ticker_line: Westpac reviews loans after negative gearing ban
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: AusSuper chair slams 'flawed' annual test
summary: Don Russell, chairman of AustralianSuper, criticises the annual performance test for super funds, calling it flawed and harmful to investment decisions. He supports proposed changes by Treasurer Jim Chalmers but believes a complete overhaul is necessary to encourage better investments for the economy.
ticker_line: Super fund test needs major overhaul, says Russell
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Buy local to build Aussie AI industry
summary: Australia is being urged to support local AI start-ups to strengthen its digital economy and ensure data sovereignty. Government officials stress that buying from Australian firms will help build expertise and competitiveness in the growing AI sector.
ticker_line: Support local AI firms to boost Australia's tech industry
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: AI

title: Australia leads rise of the small language model
summary: Australia is emerging as a leader in developing small language models (SLMs), which are more manageable and cost-effective than larger models. Businesses are increasingly recognising the benefits of creating their own SLMs using specialised data to gain a competitive edge.
ticker_line: Australia leads in small language model development
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: AI

title: CVs losing value for hiring as use of artificial intelligence changes industry, recruiters say
summary: The recruitment industry is changing as more job seekers and employers use artificial intelligence (AI), making traditional CVs less effective. Experts suggest that while AI helps in creating tailored applications, candidates must still showcase their unique skills and experiences.
ticker_line: AI reshapes job applications and recruitment process
heading: AI

title: ‘Brave assumptions’: FWC queries inflation forecasts
summary: The Fair Work Commission is questioning the government's inflation forecasts as it considers a wage rise for workers. The ACTU is pushing for a 6% increase, while the government suggests a rise below 5% could still be acceptable.
ticker_line: FWC questions government inflation forecasts for wage rise
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: Economy

title: Climate inaction to force up rates: Kean
summary: Matt Kean, Australia's climate change tsar, warns that failing to address climate change will lead to permanently higher interest rates and lower household incomes. A report shows that the economic impact of climate inaction will be severe, particularly in NSW and Queensland.
ticker_line: Climate inaction could raise interest rates permanently
author: ROSIE LEWIS, NATIONAL AFFAIRS CORRE
heading: General Insurance

title: Major bank reveals big change to branches
summary: ANZ Bank will open 27 branches on Saturdays starting June 27, 2026, to help customers who are busy during the week. The branches will offer services like home loans and specialist support, but not over-the-counter transactions.
ticker_line: ANZ Bank to open 27 branches on Saturdays
author: Jack Nivison
heading: Banking

title: IAG report reveals generational economic strain
summary: IAG's new report shows Australians are cautiously optimistic despite facing rising costs and climate issues, but younger generations feel more financial pressure than older ones. The report highlights a growing gap in resilience between different age groups and regions across the country.
ticker_line: IAG report highlights generational economic divides
author: Advertiser content for IAG
heading: General Insurance

title: NIB in stealth restructure, defence mandate change-up
summary: NIB Holdings is undergoing a major internal restructure after selling its travel division, leading to the departure of its health insurance chief, James Barr. The company has also changed its corporate defence advisor from Citi to Morgan Stanley amid a cautious outlook for its operations.
ticker_line: NIB Holdings restructures after travel division sale
author: Sarah Thompson and Kanika Sood and
heading: Health Insurance

title: Super’s ‘valley of pain’ meets Labor’s CGT trap
summary: The government's new capital gains tax changes are affecting how retirees manage their pensions and investments. A minimum 30% tax rate could encourage some retirees to collect a small pension, but it may also complicate their financial planning and investment returns.
ticker_line: New tax changes reshape retirement planning for Australians
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Superannuation

title: Actuaries Institute report shows tax system favouring older Australians
summary: A report from the Actuaries Institute shows that Australia's tax system benefits older Australians more than younger ones, leading to a significant income gap. For example, a 71-year-old with a $100,000 income has a net income of $128,100, while a 30-year-old with the same income has only $85,700 after taxes and government benefits.
ticker_line: Tax system favours older Australians over youth
author: business Gareth Hutchens
heading: Economy

title: Super funds falling short
summary: Australia's top 30 super funds have only invested 4% of the renewable energy needed to meet 2030 climate goals, contributing just $771 million to clean energy projects. Only six of these funds have direct investments in renewable energy or battery storage.
ticker_line: Super funds lag in renewable energy investments
heading: Superannuation

title: Small businesses concerned personal savings will be relied on amid payday super changes
summary: Starting July 1, 2026, businesses in Australia must pay superannuation to employees on payday instead of quarterly. While this aims to improve transparency and reduce unpaid super, many small businesses are worried about cash flow issues and may need to rely on personal savings to meet these new obligations.
ticker_line: New payday super rules raise cash flow concerns
author: business Adelaide Miller
heading: Superannuation

title: Critical CMS alert puts patching obligations under insurance spotlight
summary: A critical alert from the Australian Cyber Security Centre warns that cyber criminals are exploiting known weaknesses in content management systems, putting pressure on businesses to patch vulnerabilities or face insurance consequences. As cyber insurance uptake falls among small and medium-sized enterprises, insurers are now enforcing patch management obligations in their policies.
ticker_line: Cyber insurance faces new patching requirements
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Zest Insurance partners with Victorian Chamber on SME risk education
summary: Zest Insurance has partnered with the Victorian Chamber of Commerce to help small businesses in Victoria understand insurance and risk management through expert-led webinars and resources. This move comes as many Australian SMEs face rising insurance costs and underinsurance issues.
ticker_line: Zest Insurance teams up to boost SME risk education
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: APRA proposes prudential changes with operational impact for insurers
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) proposed changes to its rules for banks and insurers on July 10, 2026, including raising the credit conversion factor for cash advances and ensuring insurance risk charges cannot be negative. Submissions on these changes are due by August 21, 2026, with most updates expected to take effect from January 1, 2027.
ticker_line: APRA proposes key rule changes for insurers
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: Regulation

title: HeirWealth launches AI integration
summary: HeirWealth has introduced a new AI tool that allows financial advisers to easily access and query client wealth data using AI assistants like ChatGPT. This tool aims to save time and improve productivity by simplifying the process of gathering information for client advice.
ticker_line: HeirWealth launches AI tool for financial advisers
author: Vinny Vucago
heading: AI

title: ASIC Daily News round-up - 10 July 2026
summary: ASIC has cancelled the Australian financial services licence of CAIP Services Pty Ltd because the company stopped operating its financial services business. The cancellation took effect on 8 July 2026.
ticker_line: ASIC cancels CAIP Services' financial licence
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: ASIC cancels AFS licence of CFD issuer Trive
summary: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has cancelled the financial services licence of Trive Financial Services Australia Pty Ltd, effective from 1 July 2026, due to regulatory issues.
ticker_line: ASIC cancels Trive's financial services licence
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: ASIC cancels CAIP Services' AFS licence for ceasing to carry on a financial services business
summary: ASIC has cancelled the Australian financial services licence of CAIP Services Pty Ltd, effective from 8 July 2026, due to the company stopping its financial services operations.
ticker_line: ASIC cancels CAIP Services' financial licence
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: Allianz Partners Australia charts growth strategy as Europe cuts 1,800 jobs
summary: Allianz Partners is cutting 1,500 to 1,800 jobs in Europe due to AI automation, but in Australia, it plans to grow without job cuts, focusing on enhancing customer service and expanding its travel insurance business after acquiring part of nib's portfolio.
ticker_line: Allianz Partners Australia to grow, no job cuts planned
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: Embedded luxury: Carmakers' insurer partnerships reshape motor distribution
summary: Zurich Australia and Mercedes-Benz have launched a new insurance product that integrates directly into the car buying process, reflecting a trend where carmakers partner with insurers to offer tailored coverage, especially for luxury vehicles. This shift could change how insurance is sold, reducing the role of traditional brokers in the market.
ticker_line: Carmakers partner with insurers for embedded coverage
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: APRA consults on additional minor updates to the prudential and reporting framework
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is seeking feedback on minor updates to its rules for banks, insurers, and superannuation funds. The deadline for submissions is 21 August 2026.
ticker_line: APRA seeks input on minor rule updates
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: Media release: East coast gas market to be well supplied in Q4 2026, but more investment needed to meet long-term demand
summary: The ACCC reports that the east coast gas market will have enough supply in the last quarter of 2026, with a surplus of 13 petajoules expected. However, more investment is needed to ensure future demand is met.
ticker_line: East coast gas market to have surplus supply
author: ACCC Media <do-not-reply@accc.gov.au>
heading: ACCC

title: Media release: Growing home battery uptake delivering lower electricity bills, but consumer protections needed
summary: The ACCC reports that over 400,000 home batteries were installed in Australia last year, helping to lower electricity bills. However, they warn that new consumer protections are needed to tackle emerging issues in the energy market.
ticker_line: Home battery growth lowers bills, needs protections
author: ACCC Media <do-not-reply@accc.gov.au>
heading: ACCC

title: Disaster grants reveal insurers’ broader resilience challenge
summary: Suncorp and FRRR have given $431,423 to 22 community projects for disaster recovery in rural Australia. This comes as the insurance industry faces record losses from extreme weather, highlighting the need for better disaster preparedness and infrastructure investment.
ticker_line: Suncorp funds community disaster recovery projects
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Pandemic oversight of health insurers ends after five years
summary: Australia's five-year oversight of health insurers' pandemic commitments has ended, showing insurers returned more money to policyholders than they saved during COVID-19, with a total giveback of $5.058 billion against assessed savings of $4.838 billion.
ticker_line: Health insurers exceed pandemic giveback commitments
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: Health Insurance

title: Insurers push broader repair laws to curb motor claims costs
summary: Australia's insurers want the Right to Repair scheme expanded to require car manufacturers to sell parts and stop using software that restricts repairs. They argue that without these changes, repair costs will keep rising, leading to higher motor insurance premiums for consumers.
ticker_line: Insurers call for expanded Right to Repair laws
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: QBE expands risk-prevention push with business self-assessment
summary: QBE Insurance has launched online self-assessments for Australian businesses to help them identify and reduce risks before losses occur. The assessments cover areas like property, liability, motor fleet, and ESG, targeting businesses without dedicated risk teams.
ticker_line: QBE launches self-assessments for business risk management
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: ATO sector regulation review commended
summary: The SMSF Association supports a new audit by the Australian National Audit Office on the ATO's regulation of self-managed super funds, the first in nearly 20 years. The review will assess how well the ATO manages compliance and investigates non-compliance, with public input accepted until October 25, 2026.
ticker_line: ATO's SMSF regulation under performance audit
author: https://www.facebook.com/smstrusteenews
heading: Superannuation

title: SMSFA backs review of ATO's regulatory approach
summary: The SMSF Association supports a review by the Australian National Audit Office of the Australian Taxation Office's regulation of self-managed super funds, highlighting the need to ensure the rules are still effective as the sector grows.
ticker_line: SMSFA supports ATO regulation review for super funds
author: Vinny Vucago
heading: Superannuation

title: ASFA makes suggestions for performance test overhaul
summary: The Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia (ASFA) is urging the federal government to update its annual superannuation performance test. They believe the current rules are discouraging investments in important areas like housing, infrastructure, and renewable energy.
ticker_line: ASFA calls for superannuation test updates
author: Vinny Vucago
heading: Superannuation

title: Update from the Treasury
summary: The Treasury has responded to an independent review of the Australian Office of Financial Management, outlining its plans for improvements and future actions. This response was published on 9 July 2026.
ticker_line: Treasury outlines response to financial management review
author: The Treasury <no-reply@treasury.gov.au>
heading: Treasury

title: APRA publishes updated guidelines on Recognition of an External Credit Assessment Institution
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has released updated guidelines for recognising External Credit Assessment Institutions (ECAIs). These changes clarify the recognition process and now include insurers, alongside banks, in using ECAI credit ratings.
ticker_line: APRA updates guidelines for credit assessment institutions
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: ‘Silent attrition’ looms as AI reshapes client behaviour
summary: Wealth managers are warned they risk losing clients to AI tools as more investors turn to self-directed investment strategies. A report by EY highlights the need for the industry to adapt quickly to retain clients and remain relevant.
ticker_line: Wealth managers face AI-driven client shift
author: Shy-Ann Arkinstall
heading: Investments

title: Update from the Treasury
summary: The Treasury has announced a consultation on a new minimum tax for discretionary trusts, starting on 8 July 2026. This aims to ensure fairer tax contributions from these trusts.
ticker_line: Treasury consults on minimum tax for trusts
author: The Treasury <no-reply@treasury.gov.au>
heading: Treasury

title: Inverter fires put solar projects under insurer scrutiny
summary: Insurers are increasingly concerned about inverter fire risks in solar projects across Australia and New Zealand, leading to higher deductibles and stricter coverage for those without proper safety measures. Brokers are advised to document risk mitigation strategies to maintain favorable insurance terms.
ticker_line: Insurers raise concerns over solar inverter fires
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: Media release: JustAnswer to pay $10m in penalties for misleading pricing representations and misleading affiliation claims
summary: JustAnswer LLC has been ordered by the Federal Court to pay $10 million for misleading customers about its service pricing and falsely claiming it was affiliated with the Fair Work Ombudsman, following action by the ACCC.
ticker_line: JustAnswer fined $10 million for misleading claims
author: ACCC Media <do-not-reply@accc.gov.au>
heading: ACCC

title: APRA consults on amendments to superannuation data collections
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is seeking feedback on changes to how superannuation data is collected, moving from Direct to APRA (D2A) to APRA Connect. Submissions are due by 21 August 2026.
ticker_line: APRA seeks input on superannuation data changes
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: APRA publishes new guidance on points of presence reporting (ARS 796.0)
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has released new guidance for banks on how to report their co-located service channels. This aims to ensure that data is consistent and comparable across the banking industry.
ticker_line: APRA issues new reporting guidance for banks
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: NSW levy reform could expand insurance coverage
summary: A proposed change in New South Wales could remove the Emergency Services Levy from insurance premiums, potentially adding 400,000 new policies and reducing costs for many households. However, some households may end up paying more, and the final decision is still pending a report due in November 2026.
ticker_line: NSW considers removing Emergency Services Levy from insurance
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Bird flu compensation leaves business interruption insurance gap
summary: The spread of H5 bird flu in Australia highlights a gap in insurance coverage for business interruptions caused by the disease, as government compensation only covers the value of culled birds, leaving farmers vulnerable to significant financial losses. With recent outbreaks affecting multiple states, brokers warn that private insurance options are limited and becoming more expensive.
ticker_line: Bird flu exposes insurance gaps for farmers
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Domestic motor insurance: Record profit but a claims trend to fear
summary: Australian domestic motor insurance has seen record profits, with the best quarterly result of $472 million in March 2026. However, claims costs are rising faster than premiums, driven by increasing vehicle repair expenses and the growing complexity of modern cars, especially electric vehicles.
ticker_line: Record profits in motor insurance amid rising claims
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: APRA finalises reinsurance framework reforms for general insurers
summary: APRA has updated the reinsurance rules for general insurers, making it easier for them to use alternative options like catastrophe bonds. The changes will take effect on January 1, 2027, and aim to reduce costs and regulatory burdens while ensuring policyholder protection.
ticker_line: APRA reforms reinsurance rules for insurers
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: Former bankrupt coconut water CEO acquitted of ASIC charge on appeal, withdraws conviction appeal on two other charges
summary: Tim Xenos, the former CEO of FAL Healthy Beverages, has been cleared of one criminal charge by a court after appealing his conviction. He has also decided not to pursue appeals on two other charges.
ticker_line: Former coconut water CEO wins appeal against ASIC charge
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: Former WA director Joanne Pellew convicted of Corporations Act offences following ASIC investigation
summary: Joanne Pellew, a former director from Como, WA, has been found guilty of three counts of dishonestly using her position and one count of managing a corporation while disqualified. This follows an investigation by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC).
ticker_line: Former WA director convicted of serious offences
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: APRA finalises amendments to general insurance reinsurance framework
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has updated its rules on general insurance reinsurance to make it easier for insurers to access alternative reinsurance options while ensuring policyholder protection. These changes will take effect on 1 January 2027 and aim to reduce regulatory burdens and improve risk management for insurers.
ticker_line: APRA updates reinsurance rules for insurers
author: APRA web updates <subscriptions@apra.gov.au>
heading: APRA

title: Life insurance code review parks enforceability question
summary: An independent review recommends making the Life Insurance Code enforceable, but this change will be delayed for five years. In contrast, the General Insurance Code is already moving towards enforceability, highlighting a significant difference between the two sectors.
ticker_line: Life Insurance Code enforceability delayed for five years
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: Life Insurance

title: Is insurance ready for the electric vehicle boom?
summary: The rise in electric vehicle sales in Australia is outpacing the insurance industry's ability to adapt, with concerns about data, repair costs, and the longevity of new manufacturers. Premiums for electric vehicles are increasing, and there are challenges in finding qualified technicians for repairs.
ticker_line: Insurance industry struggles with EV boom
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: ASIC review provides insights into voluntary administration and deed of company arrangement outcomes
summary: ASIC has released new data on voluntary administrations and deeds of company arrangement, showing how these processes work and which companies benefit from them. This is the first time ASIC has provided such detailed insights into these financial practices.
ticker_line: ASIC reveals insights on company administration processes
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: Australia can't sustain disaster cover on the private market, report finds
summary: A report from The University of Queensland warns that Australia cannot rely solely on private insurance for disaster coverage. It highlights the need for collaboration between governments, insurers, and consumers to ensure affordable and accessible disaster insurance as costs rise.
ticker_line: Australia faces disaster insurance sustainability crisis
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Extreme heat escapes insurance losses despite mounting physical climate risk
summary: Extreme heat is the deadliest natural hazard in Australia, causing more deaths than any other disaster, yet it is largely excluded from insurance claims. New regulations require insurers to report on climate risks, highlighting the need for better preparation against heatwaves.
ticker_line: Extreme heat poses hidden risks for insurers
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Vero, GIO open 2026 risk awards as workforce risk climbs
summary: Vero Insurance and GIO Workers Compensation have opened entries for the 2026 RM Advancer Awards, highlighting the growing importance of managing workforce and mental health risks for small and medium businesses. Recent data shows a significant rise in mental health-related workers' compensation claims, prompting changes in regulations and risk management practices.
ticker_line: Vero and GIO launch 2026 risk awards amid rising claims
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: Youi neighbour survey: most disputes fall outside home cover
summary: A Youi Insurance survey found that noise is the most common neighbour dispute in Australia, but most issues are not covered by home insurance. The survey highlighted parking conflicts as the only relevant area for insurance claims, with 60% of people considering it important not to block driveways.
ticker_line: Youi survey reveals neighbour dispute trends
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/roxanne-libatique/
heading: General Insurance

title: ATO client data updated fairly promptly
summary: The ATO will update superannuation data after June 30, with key information available from July 13. Industry super funds may provide data faster than SMSFs, and clients are encouraged to set up a myGov account for easy access.
ticker_line: ATO updates super data post June 30
author: https://www.facebook.com/smstrusteenews
heading: Superannuation

title: VanEck switches to AI-powered index
summary: VanEck has changed its investment strategy to use an AI-powered index, aiming to improve performance and adapt to market changes. This shift reflects a growing trend in the finance industry towards technology-driven investment solutions.
ticker_line: VanEck adopts AI-driven investment index
author: Matthew Wai
heading: Investments

title: Australia welcomes 25k to millionaires' club: UBS
summary: A report by UBS shows that Australia has welcomed 25,000 new millionaires, bringing the total to over a million. This growth highlights the increasing wealth in the country, driven by factors such as rising property values and stock market performance.
ticker_line: Australia's millionaire count surpasses one million
author: Karren Vergara
heading: Economy

title: HESTA, Cbus, Mercer report super returns
summary: HESTA, Cbus, and Mercer reported strong superannuation returns for the financial year 2025-26, with HESTA achieving 9.46% and Cbus 9.25% due to their diversified investment strategies. Mercer’s SmartPath option delivered returns between 10% and 10.6% for younger members, highlighting the importance of resilience in uncertain markets.
ticker_line: Strong super returns despite global uncertainty
author: Riddhima Talwani
heading: Superannuation

title: ASIC Daily News round-up - 6 July 2026
summary: ASIC is asking for public feedback on its plan to remake three financial market relief instruments that help with market operations for the next five years.
ticker_line: ASIC seeks public input on market relief rules
author: "ASIC Media Unit" <media@newsletters.asic.gov.au>
heading: ASIC

title: Superannuation funds deliver double-digit returns for Aussies amid AI boom: 'Tremendous result'
summary: Superannuation funds in Australia have achieved strong returns, with many funds reporting double-digit growth, driven by international equities and the US artificial intelligence boom. The median growth fund returned about 9.5% in the 2026 financial year, marking the fourth year of above-average returns.
ticker_line: Super funds see strong returns amid AI boom
author: Tamika Seeto
heading: Superannuation

title: Retail super funds fall short of FY25 blockbuster returns
summary: Australia's retail super funds saw strong investment returns in FY26, but most fell short of the previous year's exceptional gains due to market volatility and geopolitical issues. Colonial First State led with a 12.74% return, while AMP and MLC also reported solid performances, driven by investments in artificial intelligence and global equities.
ticker_line: Retail super funds report strong but lower returns
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation

title: The insurance market cycle is dead: Long live the "micro-rating" era
summary: The traditional insurance market cycle is changing, with rates falling but risks rising. Brokers now need to focus on specific lines and regions rather than general trends, as different markets are behaving unpredictably.
ticker_line: Insurance pricing shifts to 'micro-rating' approach
author: https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/au/authors/daniel-wood/
heading: General Insurance

title: AFCA issues ICA 29 EDR response guides for insurers
