author: Luke Kinsella and Ronald Mizen
heading: Economy

title: Sharp fall in living standards
summary: A new OECD report shows Australians' living standards have sharply declined since the pandemic, with real wages down 5.1% since March 2021. High inflation is expected to cause further wage declines this year, complicating the government's economic recovery efforts.
ticker_line: Australians face sharp decline in living standards
author: Michael Read
heading: Economy

title: Banks still falling short, charging dead people, says watchdog
summary: A report by the Banking Code Compliance Committee found that three Australian banks are still not properly handling accounts of deceased customers, asking families for account details instead of searching their systems. If these banks do not improve, they may be reported to the corporate regulator.
ticker_line: Banks still charging fees to deceased customers
author: David Ross
heading: Banking

title: Downbeat Deloitte’s dire outlook
summary: Deloitte Access Economics predicts Australia's economy will face its longest period of low growth since the 1990s, with GDP expected to rise only 1.3% this financial year due to high inflation and interest rates. Business leaders warn that government policies are hurting investment and productivity, leading to concerns about future economic performance.
ticker_line: Deloitte warns of prolonged economic downturn
author: Thomas Henry, Perry Williams, Rosie
heading: Economy

title: Labor push for RBA to keep rates lower
summary: Labor party members are pushing to change the Reserve Bank's role to keep interest rates lower, as they face pressure from voters shifting to One Nation. Treasurer Jim Chalmers is managing this proposal ahead of the party's conference, while maintaining support for the bank's independence.
ticker_line: Labor seeks to lower interest rates amid voter shift
author: Paul Sakkal | Chief political corre
heading: Economy

title: Australia’s data centre explosion: Why the surge in NSW, Victoria is now eclipsing the mining boom
summary: NSW and Victoria are experiencing a massive surge in data centre investments, surpassing the previous mining boom with nearly $300 billion spent in the past year. While this growth is expected to boost the economy, concerns remain about its long-term benefits and potential risks.
ticker_line: Data centre boom outpaces mining investment in Australia
author: Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Strata giant unmasked in secret lobbying to save insurance kickbacks
summary: PICA Group, Australia's largest strata company, secretly lobbied against a ban on insurance kickbacks, claiming it would threaten 70% of strata management firms. The government has not yet banned these kickbacks but is considering further studies on the issue.
ticker_line: PICA Group lobbies against insurance kickback ban
author: Rachael Dexter
heading: Regulation

title: AI is coming for these 82 Aussie jobs
summary: Deloitte warns that 82 Australian jobs are at high risk from AI disruption in 2026, predicting job growth will slow significantly due to economic conditions and AI advancements. While some companies have already cut jobs, overall employment in affected sectors is still growing, though at a reduced rate.
ticker_line: Deloitte warns of AI job risks in Australia
author: Heath Parkes-Hupton
heading: AI

title: Victorian teachers in class action over alleged unpaid superannuation
summary: Victorian teachers, led by Jill Perkins, are suing the Department of Education for unpaid superannuation on a salary allowance from 1995 to 2023, claiming a total loss of $400 million. The Australian Education Union argues this payment should have included superannuation contributions, which the department is currently appealing in court.
ticker_line: Victorian teachers sue for unpaid superannuation
author: story by Sacha Payne
heading: Superannuation

title: Federal government accused of AI policy retreat as US tech giants plan Australian investments
summary: The Australian government has backed away from strict AI regulations, which has raised concerns about safety as major tech companies plan significant investments in the country. Critics argue that the decision could have serious implications for consumer protection and privacy.
ticker_line: Government retreats on AI safety regulations
author: Steve Cannane, Lesley Robinson, Lar
heading: Regulation

title: Closing the AI skills gap is not just a hiring problem
summary: Australian organisations need to improve AI skills within their workforce, not just hire new talent. A more integrated approach in education and ongoing training is essential to close the skills gap and effectively implement AI solutions.
ticker_line: Australia urged to boost AI skills development
author: Andrew Foo
heading: AI

title: Labor pivots to AI intervention
summary: The Albanese government plans to impose stricter rules on data centres, requiring them to benefit local communities and add to the electricity grid. This move aims to address public concerns about AI's impact on jobs and the economy ahead of the upcoming ALP National Conference.
ticker_line: Labor to enforce new rules on data centres
author: Ronald Mizen | Political correspond
heading: AI

title: Shock inflation ranking
summary: Australia's inflation rate has reached the second-highest in the world at 3.6% for the year to May, causing concerns for homeowners facing rising mortgage rates. Experts warn that the Reserve Bank of Australia may increase interest rates in August due to ongoing inflation pressures.
ticker_line: Australia's inflation hits second-highest globally
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Economy

title: Price tumbles spread to all capital cities
summary: House prices are falling across all Australian capital cities, with forecasts predicting a decline of up to 8% by the end of 2027. High interest rates and changes to property tax laws are discouraging first home buyers from entering the market during this downturn.
ticker_line: House prices drop nationwide amid economic uncertainty
author: Thomas Henry Noah Yim
heading: Economy

title: Bosses warn on ‘heavy-handed’ union AI role
summary: Australia's largest employer groups warn that Labor's new AI policies, influenced by unions, could harm productivity and competitiveness. They argue that union involvement in AI decisions may slow down business innovation and investment.
ticker_line: Employers warn unions may hinder AI progress
author: Noah Yim
heading: AI

title: ‘Can’t let it control us’: Taylor’s vow of AI vigilance
summary: Opposition Leader Angus Taylor has called for careful management of artificial intelligence to prevent job losses and protect Australian creators' rights. He believes AI can boost productivity and wages but warns against letting it dominate our lives.
ticker_line: Taylor calls for balanced approach to AI
author: Joseph Olbrycht-Palmer
heading: AI

title: ANZ’s fixed rate shock
summary: ANZ has reduced its two and three-year fixed home loan rates by up to 0.10 percentage points, with the lowest rate now at 6.29%. This move is unusual as most major banks have been increasing their fixed rates recently.
ticker_line: ANZ cuts fixed home loan rates amid rising trends
heading: Banking

title: Anthropic urges AI freeze amid fear of losing control
summary: Anthropic, the top AI company, has called for a global pause on developing powerful AI systems, warning that humanity risks losing control. They are concerned about rapid advancements that could lead to dangerous outcomes and suggest that all major AI firms should agree to slow down their work.
ticker_line: Anthropic calls for global AI development freeze
author: David Swan | Technology
heading: AI

title: Business banking on boost from AI
summary: Australian business leaders, including executives from BHP and Uber, discussed how adopting artificial intelligence (AI) could improve productivity despite some challenges. They highlighted AI's potential to prevent costly shutdowns and create new job roles, while also acknowledging the current low productivity levels in Australia since the Covid-19 pandemic.
ticker_line: AI seen as key to boosting productivity
author: Jared Lynch, Perry Williams
heading: Economy

title: The one age at which you will pay more tax than any other
summary: People aged 43 pay the most tax in Australia, averaging $59,000 a year. This age group faces high taxes due to higher incomes and family expenses, while younger and older Australians benefit more from government spending.
ticker_line: Age 43 sees peak tax burden in Australia
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: CBA warns of new hit to Australian economy
summary: Commonwealth Bank warns that falling house prices will slow Australia's economy, with GDP growth expected at just 1.5% for 2026. However, mortgage holders may benefit as the Reserve Bank is likely to keep interest rates steady for the rest of the year.
ticker_line: CBA forecasts economic slowdown due to housing market
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Economy

title: Watchdog’s data ‘points to uneven liability market’
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority's updated data shows an uneven liability insurance market, with Lloyd's stable market share but declining premiums, especially in the construction sector, where costs have tripled in six years. The report highlights ongoing affordability issues for small businesses in this industry.
ticker_line: Liability insurance market shows uneven trends
heading: General Insurance

title: Trade-offs required as risk protection gap widens, researchers say
summary: A report from the University of Queensland highlights the growing gap in disaster insurance coverage due to increasing natural disasters. It suggests that insurance needs to adapt by supporting risk reduction efforts to remain sustainable and effective for society.
ticker_line: Insurance must adapt as disaster risks rise
heading: General Insurance

title: How insurer transformation reshaped the smash repair sector
summary: The Australian smash repair sector has shifted from oversupply to potential undersupply due to changes in the insurance industry, leading to longer repair times and workforce shortages. The average repair time increased from 38.57 days in 2019 to 61.25 days in 2024, highlighting the industry's growing fragility.
ticker_line: Smash repair sector faces undersupply crisis
author: Ron Arnold
heading: General Insurance

title: Awards recognise life advice
summary: The Financial Advice Association Australia has added a new risk adviser category to its awards, highlighting the importance of life insurance guidance. Sponsored by Zurich Australia, the awards are open for submissions until July 30, with winners announced on November 26 in Melbourne.
ticker_line: New award for life insurance advisers launched
heading: Life Insurance

title: Nation's inflation rate is highest in developed world
summary: Australia's inflation rate has reached the highest levels among developed countries, with core inflation at 3.6% in May. Economists criticize the Reserve Bank for not acting decisively enough to control rising prices, complicating the government's efforts to manage cost-of-living concerns.
ticker_line: Australia's inflation rate hits developed world high
author: Michael Read
heading: Economy

title: Economist survey: No rate cuts 'until 2027' as fourth rise looms
summary: Economists predict no interest rate cuts from the Reserve Bank of Australia until at least 2027, with a possible fourth rate rise still on the table due to ongoing inflation concerns and global tensions. The cash rate is currently at 4.35%, and many believe it will remain unchanged for the rest of the year.
ticker_line: No rate cuts expected until 2027
author: Grace Lagan
heading: Economy

title: Judge rules AI evidence 'bordered on contempt'
summary: A judge has warned that using AI to gather evidence in dismissal cases can lead to serious legal issues, as seen in a case where a delivery driver relied on a chatbot that created false evidence, resulting in his case being dismissed and potential penalties for him.
ticker_line: Judge warns against AI misuse in court cases
author: David Marin-Guzman | Workplace corr
heading: Regulation

title: Market minds: Why Australia is the world's most underappreciated AI trade
summary: Australia is becoming an important player in the global AI infrastructure market, with significant investments in data centres and power supply expected to grow. Companies like NextDC and Macquarie Technology are leading this growth, while resources like copper will be crucial for supporting AI's energy needs.
ticker_line: Australia emerges as key player in AI infrastructure
author: Jun Bei Liu
heading: Investments

title: Privatisation of essential services driving cost-of-living crisis
summary: A study by the McKell Institute shows that the rising cost of living in Australia is largely due to the privatisation of essential services, with prices for utilities, health, and education increasing much faster than general inflation over the past 20 years. Experts argue that restoring public services and better regulating prices is essential to help everyday Australians manage these costs.
ticker_line: Privatisation linked to rising living costs in Australia
author: Nathan Schmidt
heading: Economy

title: RESERVE BANK RATE RISE TIPPED
summary: Top economists expect the Reserve Bank to raise the official cash rate by 25 basis points to 2.5% this week, as concerns about inflation ease. However, some banks believe the Reserve Bank should wait to give the economy more time to recover.
ticker_line: Reserve Bank rate hike expected this week
author: Tom Pullar-Strecker
heading: Economy

title: Superannuation: Risk assessed, AI craze fuels double-digit return for 'rational' funds
summary: Major Australian superannuation funds report around 10% returns for the year, driven by gains in international shares and optimism around artificial intelligence. While they acknowledge risks from high valuations, they do not believe there is a bubble in the AI sector at this time.
ticker_line: Super funds see strong returns amid AI optimism
author: Clancy Yeates
heading: Superannuation

title: Beyond AI pilots, the organisations turning ambition into action
summary: Many organisations are moving from AI experiments to real change by building strong data foundations. RACQ and RMIT are examples of how connecting data and AI can improve customer experiences and business results.
ticker_line: RACQ and RMIT lead AI transformation efforts
author: Katrina Troughton
heading: AI

title: AI sway: unions steer ALP
summary: Labor's draft policy now includes stricter rules on artificial intelligence, reflecting union demands for more oversight. The changes also reaffirm Labor's commitment to renewable energy and include tax hikes from the May budget.
ticker_line: Labor tightens AI regulations amid union pressure
author: GREG BROWN, CHIEF POLITICAL CORRESP
heading: Regulation

title: Aussies pull out $13 billion, huge super move
summary: Over the past year, Australians have withdrawn more than $13 billion from large super funds to invest in self-managed super funds (SMSFs), nearly doubling the trend from the previous year. While SMSFs offer more control over investments, they come with significant risks and responsibilities that many may not fully understand.
ticker_line: Aussies withdraw $13 billion for self-managed super funds
author: Ben Graham
heading: Superannuation

title: Treasury: No boost to growth from budget
summary: Treasury reports that the Albanese government's major tax changes won't boost economic growth or productivity, focusing instead on wealth redistribution. Despite some targeted measures for small businesses, overall forecasts for growth remain unchanged.
ticker_line: Treasury says budget won't boost economic growth
author: John Kehoe | Economics
heading: Economy

title: CBA offers staff $3000 a head to recommend private bank recruits
summary: Commonwealth Bank is offering staff $3000 for each successful referral of new hires to its private bank until June 30, as competition for skilled workers intensifies. This follows a trend of major banks, including Westpac and NAB, actively recruiting talent from each other.
ticker_line: CBA pays $3000 for staff recruitment referrals
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking

title: 'Complacent' super giants lose $8b in a year
summary: Australians moved nearly $8 billion from large super funds to self-managed super funds (SMSFs) in the past year, highlighting a growing trend of disengagement with traditional pension funds. Major funds like AustralianSuper and Australian Retirement Trust are facing increased member losses as more people seek personalized financial advice and control over their investments.
ticker_line: Australians shift $8 billion to SMSFs
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Negative equity looms for recent homebuyers
summary: Commonwealth Bank predicts falling house prices in Sydney and Melbourne, which could lead to negative equity for recent buyers. Other cities like Perth and Brisbane are expected to see price increases.
ticker_line: House price drops may hit recent buyers hard
author: David Rogers, markets
heading: Economy

title: Jobs harder to find but AI not to blame
summary: A Deloitte report shows that finding a job in Australia will be tougher in 2026, but this is not mainly due to AI. Instead, rising interest rates and economic issues from the Middle East conflict are affecting hiring.
ticker_line: Job market tightens amid economic uncertainty
heading: AI

title: Rates are taking their toll, but don’t expect quick cuts
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia has decided to keep interest rates at 4.35% as high rates are slowing the economy. They warn that inflation may take another two years to return to target levels, and further rate hikes are possible if needed.
ticker_line: RBA holds rates steady amid economic slowdown
author: Sophie Foster
heading: Economy

title: There’s one age you’ll pay more tax than any other
summary: People aged 43 pay the most tax in Australia, averaging $59,000 a year, due to high incomes and spending. Recent government budget changes are expected to increase tax burdens on younger and middle-aged Australians while benefiting older investors.
ticker_line: Age 43 sees peak tax burden in Australia
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: New super rules spark $6bn small business cash crisis
summary: New rules for superannuation payments, starting July 1, require Australian employers to pay super at the same time as wages, potentially forcing small businesses to find an extra $6 billion and dip into personal savings due to cash flow issues from late customer payments.
ticker_line: New super rules may strain small business cash flow
author: Rishab Jain
heading: Superannuation

title: Unfair health insurance rebate cut will ripple through the generations
summary: The Albanese government plans to cut health insurance rebates for older Australians, arguing for fairness between generations. However, this decision may lead to more financial and emotional strain on families as older Australians could drop their coverage, resulting in longer public hospital waiting times and increased care responsibilities for their adult children.
ticker_line: Health insurance rebate cuts impact families deeply
author: DR RACHEL DAVID
heading: Health Insurance

title: Labor’s planned NDIS overhaul is ‘blunt and inequitable’, thinktank says
summary: The Grattan Institute criticises Labor's plan to cut funding for social participation in the NDIS by 50%, calling it unfair and poorly thought out. The government aims to reduce costs in the $50 billion scheme, but the changes may harm those with disabilities who rely on these supports.
ticker_line: NDIS funding cuts face strong criticism from experts
author: Sarah Basford Canales
heading: General Insurance

title: Unexpected AI boom contributed to RBA interest rate U-turn
summary: RBA Governor Michele Bullock says the unexpected growth in artificial intelligence (AI) has boosted the global economy, leading the Reserve Bank to change its plans on interest rates. Despite earlier expectations of an economic slowdown, strong AI investment has driven growth in Australia and its trading partners.
ticker_line: AI boom prompts RBA interest rate changes
author: Lea Jurkovic
heading: Economy

title: RBA boss says economy can weather war and avoid recession
summary: Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock believes Australia can avoid a recession despite rising oil prices due to the ongoing war. She stated that the economy is expected to grow, with strong investment in sectors like software and renewable energy.
ticker_line: RBA optimistic about economy amid rising oil prices
author: Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Car theft falls in every state but one and insurers are angry
summary: Car theft claims in Victoria have surged by 25%, reaching $243 million, while other states saw declines. Insurers are upset as the rise in thefts is linked to increased crime concerns in the state ahead of the upcoming election.
ticker_line: Victoria sees major rise in car theft claims
author: Patrick Durkin
heading: General Insurance

title: 20pc house price fall will open doors for investors
summary: Morgan Stanley's chief economist Chris Read says house prices need to fall by 15-20% to attract investors back into the housing market after recent tax changes. He warns that lower prices and higher rental yields are necessary for investors to regain previous returns.
ticker_line: House prices must drop to attract investors
author: DAVID ROGERS, MARKETS
heading: Economy

title: Bourse jumps as rate fears ease
summary: Australia's sharemarket rose as hopes grew that the Reserve Bank will keep interest rates steady. BHP and Rio Tinto reached record highs, while the economy showed slower growth, reducing the chance of further rate hikes.
ticker_line: Australian sharemarket rebounds on interest rate hopes
author: Cameron Micallef, Equities
heading: Economy

title: Stagflation fears rise as OECD lowers Australian forecasts
summary: Australia faces stagflation risks as the OECD lowers economic growth forecasts, predicting inflation to reach 5% and GDP growth to slow significantly. The country is experiencing its longest per-capita recession in over 65 years, driven by rising interest rates and reduced consumer spending.
ticker_line: Stagflation fears rise as OECD cuts forecasts
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON
heading: Economy

title: Mercer Super agrees to pay $10.3m fine to ASIC
summary: Mercer Super has agreed to pay a $10.3 million fine to the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) for failing to report serious issues with member services. The fund also faces $1.2 million in legal costs and has admitted to not reporting seven internal investigations between 2021 and 2024.
ticker_line: Mercer Super hit with $10.3 million fine
author: Angelica Snowden
heading: Regulation

title: Technology: 'Dangerous' AI program to hamper the hackers
summary: Australian organisations will soon access Anthropic's powerful AI model, Claude Mythos, which can identify software flaws that hackers might exploit. This move is part of a global effort to strengthen cybersecurity for critical systems, especially in sectors like finance and infrastructure.
ticker_line: Australia gains access to powerful AI for cybersecurity
author: David Swan
heading: AI

title: Industrial relations: RBA staff knock back 9.5pc wage rise offer
summary: Reserve Bank staff have rejected a 9.5% pay rise offer, pushing Governor Michele Bullock to renegotiate with the Finance Sector Union, which is demanding higher wages and work-from-home rights. The rejection highlights concerns over wages falling behind inflation and the need for competitive pay to retain skilled workers.
ticker_line: RBA staff reject 9.5% pay rise offer
author: Michael Read
heading: Banking

title: Regulation: Chalmers taps scandal-linked former KPMG partner for APRA role
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has appointed David Bradbury, a former KPMG partner linked to an audit scandal, as deputy chair of APRA, alongside Therese McCarthy Hockey. Both appointments aim to strengthen leadership at the regulator amid increasing complexities in the financial system.
ticker_line: Chalmers appoints scandal-linked Bradbury to APRA
author: Hannah Wootton and Edmund Tadros
heading: Regulation

title: Superannuation: AusSuper tips strong year after 9.8pc return
summary: AustralianSuper's balanced fund achieved a 9.77% return for the 2025-26 financial year, driven by strong performance in international equities, particularly in companies related to artificial intelligence. New chief investment officer Shaun Manuell is optimistic about future returns but cautious about market valuations amid rising interest rates.
ticker_line: AustralianSuper reports strong 9.77% return
author: Anthony Macdonald
heading: Superannuation

title: Retail investing: Gen Z backs ETFs as AI drives frenzy
summary: Gen Z investors are increasingly turning to ETFs as they seek to invest amid rising interest in the stock market, particularly driven by AI trends. The number of new ETFs in Australia reached a record high, with a notable gender divide in investment preferences emerging among young traders.
ticker_line: Gen Z embraces ETFs amid AI investment boom
author: Gus McCubbing
heading: Investments

title: Investment losers to avoid scrutiny as part of NRF review
summary: The Labor government's $15 billion National Reconstruction Fund is under review, but changes allowing $6 billion for low-return green energy investments will not be examined. Critics question the fund's approach and its impact on taxpayers.
ticker_line: NRF review excludes low-return investment scrutiny
author: JACK QUAIL
heading: Investments

title: How the ACT can meet its tax timetable
summary: An independent review warns that the ACT government needs major changes to its tax system to meet its goal of phasing out stamp duty by 2030. The report highlights that current tax revenue plans are unlikely to achieve this target without significant adjustments.
ticker_line: ACT faces challenges in tax reform goals
author: Jasper Lindell
heading: Economy

title: Recession risk up as prices fall
summary: Australia's property market is falling, raising concerns about a possible recession this year. Economists warn that declining house prices are hurting household confidence and spending, which could lead to slower economic growth.
ticker_line: Falling property prices raise recession fears
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: Fighting against triple threat
summary: Hobart City Council has joined six other councils in Australia to support a federal climate compensation fund. This fund would help cover rising costs from climate change impacts, reducing the financial burden on local ratepayers.
ticker_line: Hobart Council backs federal climate compensation fund
author: Bridget Clarke
heading: Regulation

title: Australia's economic slowdown is just beginning, economists warn, as recession risks rise
summary: Australia's economy is slowing down, with growth at just 0.3% in early 2026 and rising unemployment. Economists warn of a possible recession as inflation remains high and consumer spending weakens.
ticker_line: Australia faces rising recession risks amid economic slowdown
author: business correspondent David Taylor
heading: Economy

title: Seniors centre leak fears
summary: Mosman Council is investigating a data breach that may have exposed personal information of hundreds of residents linked to the Mosman Square Seniors Centre. The breach occurred through a third-party software system used for community services, and an external cybersecurity firm has been hired to assess the situation.
ticker_line: Data breach at Mosman Council raises concerns
author: Jim O’Rourke
heading: AI

title: Australia gains access to powerful AI amid questions about readiness
summary: Australia has gained access to a powerful AI system called Mythos, which is designed to improve cybersecurity. The government is under pressure to act faster on AI risks and opportunities as independent MPs raise concerns about the pace of its response.
ticker_line: Australia gains access to advanced AI for cybersecurity
author: chief digital political corresponde
heading: AI

title: AI may be tightening jobs market, but some roles could see stronger demand
summary: A Deloitte report highlights that while AI is changing the job market, it may not lead to widespread job losses. Some roles, especially those requiring human skills like creativity and empathy, could see increased demand as AI complements their work.
ticker_line: AI reshapes job market, some roles thrive
author: national work Bronwyn Herb
heading: AI

title: First trade deficit since 2017 opens ‘possible’ contraction
summary: Australia has recorded its first trade deficit since 2017, prompting major banks to lower their economic growth forecasts. This could lead to the first quarterly contraction in economic activity since the COVID pandemic, with concerns over rising imports and falling exports.
ticker_line: Australia faces first trade deficit since 2017
author: Noah Yim, Matthew Cranston
heading: Economy

title: Exclusive: RBA thinks reforms will help cut inflation
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) believes that Labor's tax reforms and falling consumer confidence may lower house prices and spending, which could help reduce inflation. This comes as the government faces criticism over its housing policies and prepares for a key interest rate decision on June 16.
ticker_line: RBA sees potential inflation relief from tax reforms
author: Paul Sakkal and Shane Wright
heading: Economy

title: Wages rise, rates to follow
summary: Australia's minimum wage will rise by 4.75% to $26.44 an hour from July 1, which may lead to two interest rate hikes later this year due to ongoing inflation and low productivity. Business leaders warn this wage increase could cause financial strain and a wave of insolvencies in the hospitality sector.
ticker_line: Minimum wage rise may trigger interest rate hikes
author: Jessica Wang
heading: Economy

title: Minister eases AI burden on FWC
summary: Minister Amanda Rishworth has proposed changes to help the Fair Work Commission manage its growing workload from AI-related claims. The new bill aims to speed up processes, reduce unnecessary hearings, and improve support for workers and businesses.
ticker_line: Fair Work Commission workload eased by new bill
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: AI

title: Our super system has a new blind spot, and it could cost you thousands
summary: Australia's financial regulator, ASIC, has found serious issues with major investment platforms managing superannuation, including high fees and inadequate oversight, which could cost members thousands. Consumers are urged to be cautious and aware of their superannuation investments, especially if advised by financial planners.
ticker_line: ASIC warns of superannuation platform risks
author: Bec Wilson
heading: Superannuation

title: Super giant ART pulls back on passive strategy
summary: The Australian Retirement Trust is shifting from passive investment strategies to more active management due to concerns over market concentration, especially in technology stocks. This change aims to better protect its $207 billion portfolio against risks associated with relying too heavily on a few companies.
ticker_line: ART shifts focus to active management strategies
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: Insurance overhaul to fix hospitals
summary: The Labor government plans to simplify private health insurance by reducing the number of products and improving access to maternity and mental health services. Insurers are concerned about rising premiums and the impact on affordability for families, while hospitals welcome the proposed changes aimed at improving patient care.
ticker_line: Labor proposes overhaul of private health insurance
author: Michael Smith | Health
heading: Health Insurance

title: Brokers flag first-home buyer loan slump
summary: Aussie Home Loans reports a 20% drop in first-home buyer loan applications since the government's budget changes, raising concerns about Labor's housing policies. While some brokers see a decrease in inquiries, others report stable interest, but high interest rates and market uncertainty are affecting buyer confidence.
ticker_line: First-home buyer loan applications drop 20%
author: Noah Yim
heading: Economy

title: COSTS OF NEGATIVE EQUITY
summary: Falling home values in Australia are causing concerns about negative equity, where homeowners owe more on their loans than their properties are worth. Experts advise borrowers to assess their loans and consider building cash buffers to avoid financial stress if property values continue to decline.
ticker_line: Falling home values raise negative equity fears
author: Anthony Keane
heading: Economy

title: Stress test reveals our resilient financial system
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has reported that assets under its regulation have surpassed $10 trillion, highlighting the strength of Australia's financial system. A recent stress test showed that major banks and superannuation funds can withstand severe economic shocks, ensuring continued stability for Australians.
ticker_line: APRA reports $10 trillion in regulated assets
author: John Lonsdale
heading: Regulation

title: State makes workplace safety legally binding
summary: NSW has made workplace safety codes legally binding, requiring employers to follow specific standards or manage risks at a higher level. This change aims to improve safety and prevent workplace injuries across various industries.
ticker_line: NSW enforces new binding workplace safety laws
author: EWIN HANNAN
heading: Regulation

title: Labor senator leads call to protect workers from AI
summary: Labor Senator Tony Sheldon is leading a new group calling for stronger protections for workers as artificial intelligence (AI) becomes more common in workplaces. The group, launching at the NSW Labor conference, aims to ensure workers have a say in how AI is used, amid concerns about job losses and the need for updated regulations.
ticker_line: Labor pushes for worker protections against AI
author: Rob Harris
heading: AI

title: CommBank readies ‘Can’ brand evolution with new agency appointment
summary: Commonwealth Bank is changing its brand strategy by appointing a new advertising agency, Howatson+Company, to refresh its long-standing 'Can' campaign. This shift aims to strengthen its market leadership and adapt to evolving customer needs and technology.
ticker_line: CommBank evolves brand with new agency appointment
author: Danielle Long
heading: Banking

title: Exclusive: RBA rates Labor bad on homes
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has criticized Labor's housing policies, saying they have not improved home supply and may have raised prices. Labor's plans to increase housing availability are under scrutiny as migration rates and other factors continue to impact affordability.
ticker_line: RBA criticizes Labor's housing policies and impact
author: Paul Sakkal | Chief political corre
heading: Economy

title: CEOs get AI twins as clones step up
summary: Australian CEOs are using AI to create digital versions of themselves, helping with tasks like writing memos and managing schedules. This trend is changing corporate communications, but experts warn that human skills are still needed for effective messaging.
ticker_line: CEOs embrace AI clones for corporate tasks
author: Jared Lynch
heading: AI

title: Australia’s median wealth falls almost 7% since 2020 despite the rich getting richer, report says
summary: A new UBS report shows that while Australia's average wealth rose by 19% since 2020, the median wealth fell by nearly 7%, highlighting a growing gap between the rich and the rest of the population. Over 25,000 new millionaires were created in Australia last year, reflecting a global trend of increasing wealth inequality.
ticker_line: Australia's wealth gap widens despite rising millionaires
author: Patrick Commins Economics
heading: Economy

title: $19bn in property deals done in just six months
summary: Australian commercial property transactions reached $19 billion in the first half of 2026, a 16% increase from last year. The retail sector led with $6.1 billion in sales, while domestic buyers became more active as offshore investment declined.
ticker_line: Australian property deals hit $19 billion in H1 2026
author: Ben Wilmot
heading: Investments

title: One Nation malcontents surf the RBA's inflation wave
summary: High inflation in Australia is causing voter frustration, benefiting Pauline Hanson's One Nation party. The Reserve Bank of Australia's failure to control inflation has led to economic discontent, with many Australians expecting conditions to worsen.
ticker_line: High inflation fuels support for One Nation party
author: John Kehoe
heading: Economy

title: Financial misconduct compensation scheme faces $170 million cost blow out
summary: The government's Compensation Scheme of Last Resort faces a $170 million shortfall to support victims of financial misconduct, including those affected by the First Guardian and Shield super collapses. The scheme, which helps recover losses from insolvent financial firms, currently has only $20 million available.
ticker_line: Compensation scheme faces $170 million funding gap
heading: Superannuation

title: Westpac director Nash latest KPMG casualty
summary: Westpac director Peter Nash has resigned from the bank's board to avoid distractions linked to the KPMG scandal, where he had connections. KPMG is under investigation for alleged misuse of client documents, and Nash's departure comes as the firm appoints a new independent chair.
ticker_line: Westpac director resigns amid KPMG scandal
author: David Ross
heading: Banking

title: Loan-slide: tax reforms hit housing
summary: Loan applications are dropping sharply, raising concerns about a cooling property market due to recent tax reforms and rising interest rates. The Reserve Bank warns this slowdown could impact the overall economy, with home values already declining in major cities.
ticker_line: Property market slows as loan applications drop
author: Matthew Cranston, David Ross, Noah
heading: Economy

title: ALP, Coalition line up to deny teenage super
summary: Labor and the Coalition have rejected calls to include superannuation for teenagers in new laws, which exempt businesses from paying super for young workers under 18 who work less than 30 hours a week. The Greens plan to challenge this decision again next month.
ticker_line: Labor and Coalition deny super for teenage workers
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON
heading: Superannuation

title: Watchdog eyes ALP ‘bias’ for industry super
summary: The Coalition has asked the National Anti-Corruption Commission to investigate the Albanese government's ties with industry super funds, claiming they pressured the corporate regulator to change rules that benefit these funds in property investments. Opposition spokesman Andrew Bragg argues this gives an unfair advantage to large super funds over individual investors.
ticker_line: Coalition seeks probe into ALP's super fund ties
author: GREG BROWN, CHIEF POLITICAL CORRESP
heading: Superannuation

title: Rules targeting risk reduction in critical infrastructure sectors
summary: New rules effective June 5 require Australian critical infrastructure operators to improve risk management, including stricter cybersecurity measures and supply chain checks. These changes aim to better protect vital sectors like energy, water, and communications from evolving threats.
ticker_line: New rules enhance risk management for critical sectors
author: Tim Johnston
heading: Regulation

title: No fees at new private hospital leaves doctors divided
summary: Adeney Private Hospital in Kew offers no out-of-pocket fees for patients with Bupa or Medibank insurance, sparking debate among doctors about its impact on healthcare. While some see it as a positive change for affordability, others warn it may lead to a system that prioritises healthy patients and limits doctor independence.
ticker_line: New hospital model sparks doctor debate
author: Henrietta Cook | Health
heading: Health Insurance

title: Call for action as bed block soars 60pc
summary: Hospital bed block has increased by 60% in three years, costing the health system $1.6 billion annually. Calls are being made for the government to fund 600 transition beds to help ease the crisis and improve care for older Australians.
ticker_line: Hospital bed block crisis prompts urgent funding call
author: SARAH ISON, ROSIE LEWIS
heading: Health Insurance

title: Treasury sets out industry’s APRA levies
summary: Treasury plans to collect about $27.2 million from the general insurance industry to support the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) in the next financial year. The overall levy for APRA is estimated at $297.3 million, which includes costs for life insurance as well.
ticker_line: Treasury outlines APRA levies for insurance sector
heading: Regulation

title: Beware knock-on effects of mental health reform: EML
summary: A report from EML warns that changes to mental health support in workers' compensation could lead to more claims in income protection insurance. It calls for better coordination across Australia's support systems to prevent financial vulnerability and improve recovery outcomes.
ticker_line: EML warns of mental health reform impacts
heading: Life Insurance

title: Ombudsman slams icare over wage adjustment delays
summary: The NSW Ombudsman has criticized icare for delays in processing wage adjustments for workers' compensation, which caused financial issues for employers. The Ombudsman recommends icare apologize, offer flexible payment plans, and improve its processes.
ticker_line: Ombudsman criticizes icare for wage adjustment delays
heading: General Insurance

title: NSW confirms workers’ comp premiums
summary: NSW employers will soon get workers' compensation renewal notices, with icare confirming premium settings. The minimum premium stays at $240, and the cap on premium increases for some employers will drop from 30% to 25%.
ticker_line: NSW confirms workers' comp premium changes
heading: General Insurance

title: Perils lifts November storms loss tally
summary: Storms in Queensland and NSW last November caused insured losses of $2.94 billion, according to Swiss data company Perils. The updated figure shows an increase from the previous estimate of $2.66 billion, with most losses coming from Queensland.
ticker_line: November storms cost $2.94 billion in losses
heading: General Insurance

title: Insurers to revisit storm-hit Clifton
summary: Insurers will hold support sessions in Clifton, Queensland on June 8-9 for residents affected by a hailstorm last November. Policyholders can meet with insurer representatives to get help with claims and recovery.
ticker_line: Insurers offer support for storm-hit Clifton residents
heading: General Insurance

title: Industry profit down amid investment slump
summary: The life insurance industry reported a profit of $121 million for the March quarter, a significant drop from $332 million a year earlier, due to lower investment returns and increased claims. Underwriting profits also fell, with individual disability insurance performing best among product classes.
ticker_line: Life insurance profits decline amid investment slump
heading: Life Insurance

title: Housing price slump in Melbourne continues
summary: Melbourne and Sydney are seeing significant drops in housing prices due to high interest rates and new tax changes. In May, Sydney house values fell to $1.58 million, while Melbourne's median house price dropped to $958,000.
ticker_line: Housing prices continue to fall in Melbourne and Sydney
author: Shane Wright and Matthew Knott
heading: Economy

title: Retirement anxiety grows
summary: Many Australians are delaying retirement by up to four years due to rising living costs, with most expecting to work until 66 instead of their desired 62. A new report shows that people now believe they need over $1 million in superannuation for a comfortable retirement.
ticker_line: Australians delay retirement amid rising costs
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Superannuation

title: Australia:APRA publishes findings of inaugural System Risk Stress Test
summary: APRA's first System Risk Stress Test shows that Australia's banks and superannuation funds can handle severe financial shocks. However, it also reveals vulnerabilities that need addressing as the superannuation sector grows.
ticker_line: APRA stress test shows financial system resilience
author: Jimmy John
heading: Banking

title: Super balances would drop 25pc in a time of severe stress: APRA
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) warns that in a severe economic crisis, superannuation funds could sell nearly $100 billion in shares and bonds, leading to a 25% drop in average member balances. This stress test highlights the risks posed by the concentration of power among a few large funds in the superannuation sector.
ticker_line: APRA warns of super fund risks in crisis
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation

title: The main tax, super and other financial changes that start today
summary: Starting July 1, 2026, tax cuts, increased super contribution limits, and changes to parental leave and Medicare thresholds take effect, benefiting workers and families. Key changes include a tax cut for those earning over $45,000, higher super caps, and a boost in paid parental leave to 130 days.
ticker_line: Major tax and super changes effective July 1
author: Rebecca Pike
heading: Economy

title: Stand up to US on AI, says Turnbull
summary: Former Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull urges Australia to demand access to advanced AI models from the US, especially after OpenAI appointed his former adviser Peter Anstee to lead its national security policy in the Asia Pacific. This comes as the US restricts AI access for allies, prompting concerns over cybersecurity and regional technology independence.
ticker_line: Turnbull calls for AI access from US
author: Paul Smith | Technology
heading: AI

title: Cost of comfortable retirement reaches a record high
summary: The cost of a comfortable retirement has risen to $80,000 a year for couples, prompting the government to assist over 2.5 million Australians planning to retire in the next decade. New superannuation reforms aim to help retirees manage their savings better as many fear their funds won't last.
ticker_line: Retirement costs soar, government steps in to help
author: Nina Hendy
heading: Superannuation

title: Super boost for teens
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers may allow over 500,000 teenagers to receive up to $1 billion in superannuation, as the Greens push to stop companies from avoiding super payments for under-18s working less than 30 hours. Critics say this loophole has cost teens around $405 million in super contributions last year.
ticker_line: Teenagers could gain $1 billion in superannuation
author: Ria Pandey
heading: Superannuation

title: APRA - Response to NCPD non-confidentiality and publications consultation
summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) will move forward with a non-confidentiality determination for the National Claims and Policies Database (NCPD), allowing for a new publication format that includes separate data for cyber insurance and management liability. The first updated publications will include data up to December 2024, with further improvements in usability and timeliness expected.
ticker_line: APRA updates NCPD publication format and data
publication: Australian Government News and Documents via PUBT
heading: Regulation

title: Minutes point to another RBA rate rise in August
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia is likely to raise interest rates in August to control inflation, which is currently at 4%. The RBA has already increased rates three times this year and expects inflation to remain above target for the next two years.
ticker_line: RBA hints at August rate hike to curb inflation
author: Duncan Evans
heading: Banking

title: WIPEOUT OF JIM’S ‘TINY TAX’ RELIEF
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers' new tax cut of $268 a year is overshadowed by rising mortgage costs and inflation, with rates expected to increase again soon. Homeowners are feeling the squeeze as property values in Sydney drop significantly, raising concerns about affordability and market confidence.
ticker_line: Tax relief overshadowed by rising mortgage costs
author: Jessica Wang, Aidan Devine & Duncan
heading: Economy

title: Rate hikes having ‘expected effect’: RBA
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) reports that recent interest rate hikes are having the expected effects on the economy, with signs of easing housing demand and inflation pressures. The RBA plans to maintain the current cash rate of 4.35% while monitoring economic conditions.
ticker_line: RBA sees expected effects from rate hikes
heading: Economy

title: Westpac audit chair sat in on pitch talks despite KPMG conflict fears
summary: Westpac director Peter Nash attended pitch meetings for the bank's $32 million audit despite concerns about conflicts of interest due to his past role at KPMG. This has raised questions about Westpac's earlier claims that he was not involved in the tender process.
ticker_line: Westpac audit pitch raises conflict of interest concerns
heading: Banking
